speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 12, 1995. The survivor is Paul Strassmann, S-T-R-A-S-S-M-A-N-N. The interviewer is Eli Epstein, E-P-S-T-E-I-N. The city is New Canaan, Connecticut. The country is the United States of America. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is May 12, 1995. We are interviewing Paul Strassmann. My name is Eli Epstein. We are in New Canaan, Connecticut. The language is English. Hello. Could we start by asking your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am Paul Strassmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-T-R-A-S-S-M-A-N-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My date of birth is January 24, 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Trencin, Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me a little bit about Trencin? What kind of town was it? How many people live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trencin, since early in the 12th century, has been the district town from which the surrounding villages derive trade, support, legal services, courts. All the institutions were always in Trencin. The military has always been in Trencin. In the '30s, it was a town of about 8,000, maybe 9,000. It was a small town nestled against a big fortress, which was dominating the town, which was built around the fortress. The fortress [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] goes back, perhaps, to the eighth century-- has been rebuilt over and over again. And the Jewish community in Trencin had a large synagogue because the Jewish population provided much of the trade and services for the surrounding countryside.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had your family been living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was-- has been living in Trencin since the late 1890s, maybe 1885.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your father or mother born in Trencin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Neither my father nor my mother were born in Trencin. They were born-- my father was born in [NON-ENGLISH], which is a small village not too far from Trencin. And my mother was born in Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your family life. How many siblings were you? What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one sister. She's still alive. She still lives in Trencin. She never left Trencin. So there's been this long continuity in there. My father was a young lieutenant who retired from the Austro-Hungarian Army in 1918 and established a small business, a food business, a food distribution business. And he just grew that business during the '30s, during the prosperity of the Czechoslovak Republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this a business he had started because of his experience in the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went into the First World War as the commander of the local battalion and created close ties with men who were his non-commissioned officers from the surrounding villages. When he returned from the First World War, he financed the establishment of a retail distribution chain, which was headed in each of these villages by his previous non-commissioned officers. And in this relationship, they built together a business, which flourished a great deal all the way to the-- to 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family comfortable or even affluent in your years growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my family started very modestly. My mother was the daughter of a carpenter. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was one of the few Jewish manual tradesmen. He was a cabinet maker, making very fine cabinetry. My maternal grandmother was-- had a very small store for selling patterns for laces. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My parental grandfather was a small innkeeper. So my parents came from very modest circumstances. But in the '30s, after my father built a brand new house all out of concrete, we were considered to be very prosperous. And certainly, my father was considered one of the prominent citizens in town. As a matter of fact, he was the first Jew elected to the District Council, which was a-- sort of a quasi-legislative body that governed the commercial and many other local relationships that were delegated to the district for, for jurisdiction.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you grow up in a family that had a religious life as such?

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my father was modestly religious. But my mother was kosher, strictly kosher. We had in our house, milchig and fleishig dishes. Kosher was carefully and meticulously followed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We attended shul every Saturday. My grandfather saw to it that I attended shul. And we had a very intense religious life by the standards of that period. Although I must say, that there was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a, a more Hasidic group also in Trencin, who considered the so-called Orthodox Jews-- and we were Orthodox Jews-- as, as being slightly assimilated because we did not wear distinctive clothing or didn't have payos. But we had a very intensive religious life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So holidays and the Sabbath were observed with family and perhaps even extended family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, family. Sabbath was observed. The candles were lit on Friday night. And my mother blessed the challah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Seders were a very important occasion. And we did not shortcut the Haggadah at all. I mean, we went through the whole thing in great detail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what it was like? Did you have family members coming from beyond just your immediate family who would attend these Seders with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was sort of considered the patriarch of the family. He had three sisters. They married men who held small shops, also in the neighborhood, the neighboring villages. And the visitations-- there were continual visitations, back and forth, to the family. As a matter of fact, I remember spending several summers with the aunts in the villages and staying over and getting to know my cousins. We, we had cousins. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every, every family had children. And and so the family visited very much. And they were also economically dependent on my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, so I think that the family life was very intense and very dependent on each other. There was no Social Security. You, you depended on your family to, to keep everybody economically viable. Who else besides your father worked in the business that he established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother worked with my father. She, she kept-- she was the cashier of the business. And she kept the same hours he did. So my mother was a working mother. And that was very much the pattern in Jewish families, trade families that you lived upstairs on top of your store. And, and you closed down the business for lunch for a long siesta. You started-- usually the stores opened at 7 o'clock in the morning. At 12 o'clock-- the store closed down from 12:00 to 2:00. We had lunch. My father took a nap. And then they went back to the store until about 8 or 9 o'clock at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your education? Where, where did you go to school for your first elementary school years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Trencin, because of the substantial Jewish community, there was a Jewish school. You would call it, by American standards, a Jewish parochial school. The school was adjoining the synagogue. There were three classrooms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the first classroom was for the first two grades. The second classroom was for the third grade. And the third classroom was for the fourth, fifth, and sixth grade. And the children pretty well knew one another from the synagogue and the classroom. And there was a very intense communication among these children, which continues to this day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have much exposure to the non-Jewish world around you in Trencin?

speaker: interviewee
text: By and large, as a child, all of my social contact, particularly when I was in Jewish parochial grade school, was with other Jewish children, although, from time to time when my father took me visiting to the villages where his men were having their stores and their distribution, I visited with them and with their children. Also, we had employees in our store who were Gentile. And I got to know them pretty well, including some of my father's chief assistants. So I always had a great deal of exposure to, to people outside of the Jewish community. But on an intense personal level, it was largely an introspective Jewish experience, particularly during my early youth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any exposure to anti-Semitism from anywhere at that point? We're speaking about the early years as a child growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, surely, because we were quite active as children. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] flowing through Trencin is a river where we used to go to swim. And, you know, once in a while, some other children threw stones at us and shouted Jew, Jew! But we, we, we considered this, you know, as natural as mosquitoes. And, and it was not very great apprehension. I mean, nobody got mugged. Nobody got badly beaten up. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I must also say that from my early youth, I was member of a fairly militant Jewish group. And we made sure that nobody got really mugged because we always went together as a fairly, fairly well-organized [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] troop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which group was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The troop that I got associated with, I think, at the age of six, was Hashomer Hatzair, which was a very active group in Trencin. Hashomer Hatzair had a little house in the-- in the-- in the working district of Trencin. We had meeting rooms there, but also there were bedrooms for those who were preparing for the aliyah to Israel. The, the Hashomer Hatzair building also had the schlichim from Israel who were professional organizers who came in and who supervised the, the, the young people who were learning a trade. By and large, the young teenagers, Jewish teenagers in Hashomer Hatzair--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bless you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --excuse me-- who were preparing for aliyah were learning a trade. And they were apprenticed to, to the various trade groups in Trencin, particularly in machine building and the repair of agricultural machinery. I, I must also mention that, that in 1940, when I could not go to school anymore, my father put me to work as an apprentice for-- to a machine shop, which repaired diesel engines that drove threshing machines. And so during the war from 1940 to 1941, I was following the track of those who were preparing for the aliyah. My father always believed that an educated Jew should also have a trade. And I continue this with my children, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back for a moment to Hashomer Hatzair. What, what do you recall were the messages, the indoctrinations that they were trying to give to the youth of Trencin? Do you remember any, any significant ideologies or messages they were trying to transmit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I would say that the Hashomer Hatzair, as I remember it, was one of militant, aggressive Zionism. Not a terrorist Zionism, but a Zionism that wanted to create a State of Israel and create a non-alienated Jewish state where anti-Semitism and the long diaspora could be terminated in, in freedom, and so the Jews could start building their own existence. I, I vaguely remember, at the age of seven, one of the big ideological props of Hashomer Hatzair was a so-called Borochov Pyramid. I don't know whether you remember this. That's, that's part of the lore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Borochov Pyramid show that every nation, every group of people built a state based on workers and, and, and head tradespeople just at the, the pinnacle. In other words, it was a socialist, kind of a worker-state orientation. The Hashomer Hatzair view was that part of the reason why Jews were so alienated and, and also subject to anti-Semitism that their social structure made them especially vulnerable in an environment because Jews were basically an inverted pyramid. On the top of the Jews were the tradesmen and the innkeepers, who were hated. And there was no working class to support it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Hashomer Hatzair view was one of leftist, socialist [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of inclination, which presumed that a socialist future was the right future for a Zionist state in Israel. And the militant part of the message, did that come across? And if so, how did it differ from, say, a Betar message?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Betar-- there was a Betar and an Irgun organization. I mean, you, you name it, we had it in Trencin. I mean, [LAUGHS] there was just a handful of Jews. But there were at least as many factions as there were Jews. This may be an exaggeration, but nevertheless. But there was a very strong Betar group. The, the, the Betar group were, were older. They were in their 20s. And they wore jackboots. They were black boots just like the storm troopers. And they, they try to, to acquire arms and prepare themselves for, for, for aggressive takeover of Israel. The Hashomer Hatzair thing was really based on the kibbutz, on the collective of kibbutz, and going back to an agricultural base, which was not-- was not the ideology of Betar. I mean, there was no underlying economic or social ideology of Betar other than aggressive takeover and seizing the land. And I guess that was one of the distinctions that I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first sense apprehension, anxiety? Events in Europe were taking place in-- certainly by the late '30s. By 1939, Slovak declared independence. But when did you first begin to sense that something was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we, we started sensing the threat maybe as early as '36 but surely in '37, when a stream of refugees started coming from Germany. My house, my father's house was always a way, way station for people going around for various causes. My mother was the chairman of WIZO, which is the woman's Zionist organization. My father's partner was, in fact, a delegate to the Jewish Congress. So there has always been a very heavy Zionist traffic through our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Incidentally, do you remember his name? Your father's partner's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Andrew Kubicek, K-U-B-I-C-E-K. He was a delegate to the Zionist Congress. And so, so, we already knew in '37 what was happening in Germany. And, and the prospects and the future of what would happen to us was widely debated at dinner table. And I remember a particularly tearful incident where one of my father's best friend divorced his wife because he wanted to leave Trencin, and she didn't want to. And that was a totally unheard-of experience. And, in fact, the scene happened in our store after the store closed in the evening. And I remember it the way it was today.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the scene between this lovely woman-- her name was Sylvia Reidlich, and her husband, Robert Reidlich-- how they came to my father, sort of to, to try to settle their dispute. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Reidlich just wanted to leave because he felt that Czechoslovakia would be taken over by Hitler. He was also depressed. And he just wanted to go somewhere. It wasn't clear where he wanted to go. He, he just wanted to leave. Now, whether that was part of marital problems or what, you know, as a child, I had no way of knowing it. I guess I must have been, by that time, eight years old. But I remember that as one of the early traumatic experiences because it started affecting family life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And yet your family stayed. Did your father not give some thought himself to alternatives and moving elsewhere? Certainly, certainly Zionism must have been positioned as an alternative route.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father-- who, by the way, spoke reasonable English and traveled widely-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] started preparing for emigration. We got passports in, I believe, as early as 1937, surely in 1938. I remember about the passports because I was having ice cream. And I was told to wash my face because they wanted to take my pictures for the passport. And I'm sure it must have been either in '37 or '38. So, so I know. You know, it was traumatic, you know, you take pictures for a passport. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father started putting money away because the idea was, well, when things really get bad, then we can always scoot out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was a very interesting incident, by the way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was putting away money, dollars in pound sterling, in bills. And since my mother was the treasurer-- so he'd always give it to my mother whenever he could. It was put into a metal cigar box. And my mother just went upstairs-- and we had sort of an attic-- and hid it and just kept doing that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then one day, I think it was early in '39-- and there was quite a bit of money there, I understand. My father said, well, I, I really need the money because I-- my-- Mr. Kubicek was going to go to the Zionist Congress in either Basel-- I think it was in Basel in 1939. He was the delegate. And the idea was that he was going to go out, take the money out, get the visa, and we will all go out. My mother goes upstairs, opens the cigarette box, which was a metal box, and all the money was charred, fell apart. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] She put the cigar box behind a radiator, you know, sort of tucked it away. Apparently, when she put it in, it didn't fall down, it stayed up. And it was the heat from the steam radiator. The only thing that I remember, and I tell it to my children, is my father didn't say a word of reproach to my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But Kubicek went out to Basel-- yeah, it was Basel; it was the Zionist Congress in Basel-- and started making arrangements. Of course, we really didn't have money by that time. Also, my father was very much torn because his parents were still living. And they would not move. They just absolutely refused. And then he had sisters and all kinds of obligations. It was one of these things where, well, let's postpone it for another month. I mean, it was never this absolute feeling of urgency. OK, now we cut it and go. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was this continual negotiation, which is a theme that very much emerges in my life in my observation of the Holocaust. Continually arguing, well, it can't be that bad. Well, maybe I'll do this, maybe I'll compromise on that. Maybe it won't be that way. And besides, I don't know what's on the other side-- this continual [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dialogue back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think you refer to it someplace as incremental rationalization.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I, I mention it to you, Eli, as an incremental rationalization, which, to me, is one of the very dominant themes of-- that lead me to explain lots of things that I've observed in the Holocaust, that, that people, for a number of reasons, were continually forced to incrementally rationalize whatever position they took, although that incremental rationalization was a one-way street towards destruction.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just going back for a moment, when, when you had your passports taken and thought was then, obviously, articulated about moving elsewhere--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to where would you have moved? Was there a discussion about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh there was discussion. And, and, of course, it was Israel, although Israel was very unsettled. And, of course, there was problems with aliyah. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had people who came who offered us to go to Madagascar. Then there was this Romanian who came and who was selling Cuban visas. I mean, there was all sorts of very interesting things. But they were always rationalized and argued about, never very conclusively.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at, at some point, you obviously-- things-- events started to turn. That--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would that be about 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was-- I was in Hashomer Hatzair at camp in the High Tatras in September 1, 1939, when the war broke out. And we actually could hear the German guns destroying the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to end the first tape now. And we'll resume on that theme as we pick up on a second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
